---
title: "Digital hardware"
description: "Logic, state, timing, verification, architecture, and programmable hardware."
page-role: part
part-id: digital
---

Digital abstraction is powerful because a range of analog states can represent
one logical value. It is trustworthy only while electrical levels, timing,
clocking, reset, and uncertainty remain within their contracts.

## Part question

How are values, decisions, state, and computation built from physical circuits,
and how do we prove that the intended behavior holds across time and inputs?

## Entry knowledge

The part assumes transistor switching, RC behavior, and disciplined use of
units and evidence. [M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd) supports
binary arithmetic and Boolean manipulation; [T02](../appendices/t02-tools-version-control.qmd)
supports executable models, tests, and versioned HDL.

## Learning arc

| Chapters | Development |
|---|---|
| D01–D02 | encode information and construct combinational decisions and arithmetic |
| D03 | introduce state, clocks, setup/hold constraints, reset, and clock-domain risk |
| D04 | express hardware and verify behavior before implementation |
| D05–D06 | assemble memory and processors, then partition systems onto programmable logic |

## Exit capability

You should be able to move between a logic requirement, truth or state model,
timed interface, HDL implementation, and verification evidence. You should also
be able to distinguish a functional error from an electrical, reset, clocking,
or timing failure.

Labs L09–L10 connect timed measurements to simulation and FPGA implementation.
The resulting models feed directly into embedded systems and the secure
connected-node study; see the
[practice and project spine](../roadmap.qmd#practice-and-project-spine).
